title: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) in a 34-Year-Old Combat Veteran Presenting to Outpatient Mental Health: Recognizing the Four DSM-5 Symptom Clusters, Screening for Suicide/Substance-Use Comorbidity, Delivering TRAUMA-INFORMED Care, Teaching GROUNDING/Coping for Flashbacks and Hyperarousal, and Connecting to First-Line Trauma-Focused Psychotherapy and SSRI/Prazosin (NOT Long-Term Benzodiazepines)
subtitle: POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) develops after EXPOSURE to actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence — by directly experiencing it, witnessing it, learning it happened to a close family member/friend, or repeated/extreme exposure to aversive details (e.g., first responders, combat). DSM-5 describes FOUR SYMPTOM CLUSTERS: (1) INTRUSION — recurrent involuntary distressing MEMORIES, NIGHTMARES, FLASHBACKS (dissociative reliving as if the event is happening again), and intense psychological/physiological distress to internal or external CUES/TRIGGERS that resemble the trauma; (2) AVOIDANCE — persistent effort to avoid trauma-related thoughts/feelings AND external reminders (people, places, conversations, activities, situations); (3) NEGATIVE ALTERATIONS in COGNITION and MOOD — inability to recall key features of the event, persistent negative beliefs about oneself/others/the world, distorted blame or GUILT, persistent negative emotional state (fear, horror, anger, shame), markedly diminished interest (anhedonia), feeling DETACHED/ESTRANGED from others, and an inability to experience positive emotions; (4) ALTERATIONS in AROUSAL and REACTIVITY — irritability/angry outbursts, reckless or self-destructive behavior, HYPERVIGILANCE, an exaggerated STARTLE response, problems with CONCENTRATION, and SLEEP disturbance. By definition the symptoms last MORE THAN 1 MONTH and cause significant DISTRESS or functional IMPAIRMENT (work, relationships); when the same picture lasts LESS than 1 month after the trauma it is ACUTE STRESS DISORDER. A dissociative subtype (depersonalization/derealization) may occur. ASSESSMENT + SAFETY: PTSD carries HIGH COMORBIDITY with DEPRESSION, SUBSTANCE/ALCOHOL USE DISORDER (often self-medication), and SUICIDE — so the nurse SCREENS for suicidal/self-harm ideation, substance use, and CURRENT SAFETY (e.g., ongoing abuse/intimate-partner violence) FIRST, and may use validated tools (e.g., the PCL-5). TRAUMA-INFORMED CARE is the framework for ALL interactions (SAMHSA principles: SAFETY; TRUSTWORTHINESS/transparency; PEER support; COLLABORATION/mutuality; EMPOWERMENT, VOICE and CHOICE; and cultural/historical/gender sensitivity) — it shifts the question from 'what is WRONG with you?' to 'what HAPPENED to you?', actively AVOIDS RE-TRAUMATIZATION, gives the client CONTROL and CHOICE, provides PREDICTABILITY (explain before doing, respect personal space, avoid surprising or cornering), and minimizes coercive/forced interventions. THERAPEUTIC COMMUNICATION: build TRUST with a calm, nonjudgmental, accepting presence; VALIDATE feelings; let the client set the PACE and do NOT force them to recount the traumatic event in detail before they are ready (a forced, premature trauma narrative can re-traumatize); do NOT MINIMIZE ('at least you survived,' 'just move on') or claim to know exactly how they feel. For FLASHBACKS, dissociation, or acute anxiety, teach and use GROUNDING techniques — the 5-4-3-2-1 sensory method, paced/diaphragmatic breathing, and ORIENTING to the present ('you are safe, you are here, it is [today], the event is over') — to reconnect the client to the here-and-now. TREATMENT: FIRST-LINE is trauma-focused PSYCHOTHERAPY — Trauma-Focused CBT, Prolonged Exposure (PE), Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT), and Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR). First-line PHARMACOLOGY is an SSRI (sertraline, paroxetine — FDA-approved) or SNRI (venlafaxine); PRAZOSIN (an alpha-1 antagonist) is used for trauma-related NIGHTMARES and sleep disturbance. BENZODIAZEPINES are generally AVOIDED — they do NOT treat the core disorder, carry dependence/misuse risk (especially with comorbid substance use disorder), can impair the processing/extinction central to recovery, and may WORSEN outcomes; alcohol/illicit substances should be reduced (they worsen symptoms and sleep). HEALTH PROMOTION/RECOVERY: provide PSYCHOEDUCATION that normalizes symptoms as an understandable RESPONSE to trauma (reducing shame/self-blame and supporting engagement), encourage SLEEP hygiene, regular EXERCISE, SOCIAL/PEER support (e.g., veterans' groups), stress-management/relaxation skills, limiting alcohol/caffeine, and gradual return to function; screen at-risk populations (veterans, abuse/assault survivors, first responders) and connect to RESOURCES and follow-up. NURSING ROLE: recognize the four symptom clusters; screen for safety/suicide/substance comorbidity FIRST; deliver trauma-informed, nonjudgmental care that gives control/choice and avoids re-traumatization; teach grounding/coping for flashbacks and hyperarousal and stabilize sleep; facilitate evidence-based treatment (trauma-focused psychotherapy +/- SSRI/SNRI, prazosin for nightmares — NOT long-term benzodiazepines); and provide psychoeducation, peer support, and recovery-oriented follow-up.
scenario: POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) develops after EXPOSURE to actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence — by directly experiencing it, witnessing it, learning it happened to a close family member/friend, or repeated/extreme exposure to aversive details (e.g., first responders, combat). steps.q: Which of the following findings is MOST important for the nurse to recognize in this client?

opts.text: Pressure him to recount every detail of the traumatic event in the first session 'to get it all out,' even as he becomes distressed
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Forcing a detailed trauma narrative before the client is ready can RE-TRAUMATIZE him; let him set the pace and build safety/trust first.

opts.text: Intrusion symptoms — recurrent nightmares, intrusive memories, and flashbacks (dissociative reliving) with intense distress to trauma-related cues (loud noises, crowds)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — intrusion is a core DSM-5 PTSD cluster. RN role: recognize intrusion symptoms.

opts.text: Since he 'survived and looks fine,' reassure him the symptoms aren't real and tell him to just move on
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Minimizing/dismissing symptoms is invalidating and erodes trust; PTSD is real and treatable. Validate and provide trauma-informed support.

steps.q: Which statement BEST explains the significance of this client’s clinical findings?

opts.text: Avoidance and hypervigilance are simply character weakness that resolve with willpower, so no treatment is needed
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + stigmatizing. PTSD is a treatable disorder, not weakness; symptoms respond to trauma-focused therapy and appropriate medication.

opts.text: PTSD develops after exposure to actual/threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence and features four DSM-5 clusters (intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, arousal/reactivity) lasting >1 month
opts.rat: CORRECT — etiology + the four clusters + timeframe. Foundation for recognition.

opts.text: Long-term benzodiazepines are the preferred first-line treatment for PTSD and should be started immediately
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Benzodiazepines are generally AVOIDED in PTSD; first-line is trauma-focused psychotherapy +/- an SSRI/SNRI (prazosin for nightmares).

steps.q: Which is the MOST likely explanation for this client’s presentation?

opts.text: Connect him to evidence-based treatment — trauma-focused psychotherapy and appropriate medication (SSRI/SNRI; prazosin for nightmares)
opts.rat: #4 — Definitive treatment. Linkage to therapy/medication drives recovery once safety and rapport are established. RN role: connect to treatment.

opts.text: Teach grounding/coping skills for flashbacks and hyperarousal and help stabilize sleep
opts.rat: #3 — Stabilizing. Grounding and sleep give the client immediate, usable tools to manage symptoms. RN role: coping skills + sleep.

opts.text: Build a trauma-informed, nonjudgmental therapeutic relationship — provide safety, trust, control, choice, and predictability
opts.rat: #2 — High. A trusting, trauma-informed relationship enables all further assessment and care and avoids re-traumatization. RN role: therapeutic relationship.

opts.text: Establish safety — screen for and address suicidal/self-harm risk, substance use, lethal-means access, and current danger
opts.rat: #1 — Safety is always first; PTSD's high suicide/substance comorbidity makes risk assessment the priority. RN role: safety first.

steps.q:
Screen for suicidal/self-harm ideation, substance/alcohol use, and lethal-means access (firearms), and assess current safety, before deeper trauma work

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: Safety-first screening for a high-comorbidity disorder. RN role: assess safety/suicide/substance use first.

steps.q:
Provide psychoeducation, connect him to a veterans' peer-support group, and counsel sleep hygiene, exercise, and limiting alcohol/caffeine, with structured follow-up

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: Health promotion/recovery support sustains outcomes. RN role: psychoeducation + peer support + lifestyle + follow-up.

steps.q:
Advise him to manage his symptoms and insomnia by drinking alcohol to relax and fall asleep

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — alcohol worsens PTSD symptoms, sleep, and risk and fuels comorbid substance use; counsel reduction and teach healthy coping.

steps.q: Which action should the nurse take FIRST?

opts.text: Teach and practice grounding/coping for flashbacks and hyperarousal (orient to present, 5-4-3-2-1, paced breathing) and address sleep
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Grounding and sleep stabilization give immediate, usable tools. RN role: coping skills + sleep.

opts.text: Facilitate referral to trauma-focused psychotherapy and collaborate on medication (SSRI/SNRI; prazosin for nightmares), avoiding long-term benzodiazepines
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Linkage to definitive, evidence-based treatment drives recovery. RN role: connect to therapy + appropriate medication.

opts.text: Assess and ensure safety first — screen for suicidal/self-harm ideation, substance use, lethal-means access, and current danger, and safety-plan as needed
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Safety/suicide-risk assessment precedes all other care in a high-comorbidity disorder. RN role: safety first.

opts.text: Establish a trauma-informed therapeutic relationship — calm, nonjudgmental, giving control/choice, explaining before doing, and letting him set the pace
opts.rat: #2 — Second. A trusting, trauma-informed relationship enables assessment and treatment and avoids re-traumatization. RN role: therapeutic relationship.

steps.q:
He engages in trauma-focused therapy (CPT) and a veterans' peer-support group; avoidance decreases and function at work and home improves

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: Engagement in first-line trauma-focused therapy + peer support driving recovery. Improving.

steps.q:
Early in care, before therapy and medication take effect, his symptoms are present and essentially unchanged while rapport, safety, and coping skills are being built

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: Before trauma-focused therapy and medication take effect, an unchanged baseline is expected; continue building safety, rapport, and coping and reassess.

steps.q:
After learning the 5-4-3-2-1 grounding method and paced breathing, he aborts a flashback in clinic, reorients to the present, and his heart rate and blood pressure settle

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: Grounding successfully manages a flashback and hyperarousal. Expected, desired response.
